title: HTTP Semantics, Roles, Representations, and Layered Evidence · Automation and Data Client Systems · Contract Workshop
summary: A civic-data automation team integrating a staging catalog must build a first protocol model from narrated fixtures and explain each policy boundary for http semantics, roles, representations, and layered evidence, producing protocol evidence map. Planned artifact: Build a complete first version of a Python 3.14 urllib and HTTP client layers protocol evidence map while every earlier competency remains active. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal d…
objectives: Distinguish client, server, user agent, origin, intermediary, gateway, proxy, and cache roles per exchange. Demonstrate the result through Python 3.14 urllib, http.client, ssl, typed protocols, and fixture transports.

Separate a resource from the selected representation and its metadata, content, and current validity. Demonstrate the result through Python 3.14 urllib, http.client, ssl, typed protocols, and fixture transports.

Trace request construction, routing, transport, intermediary handling, response interpretation, and cleanup as distinct stages. Demonstrate the result through Python 3.14 urllib, http.client, ssl, typed protocols, and fixture transports.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

starterFiles.python:
"""http-clients-python cumulative safe data-client portfolio.

Browser labs use deterministic request and response models only. Live urllib,
DNS, TLS, sockets, concurrency, and load require local Python 3.14 transfer gates.
"""

from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass(frozen=True)
class RequestEvidence:
    method: str
    target: str
    attempt: int

@dataclass(frozen=True)
class ResponseEvidence:
    status: int
    body_bytes: int
    valid: bool

print("Python HTTP client portfolio ready")

content.title: Evidence defense
content.text: Use the case, one assumption, one observable result, and one limitation. Do not copy the competency statement: Classify name resolution, connection, TLS, protocol, status, representation, and business failures without collapsing them. Demonstrate the result through Python 3.14 urllib, http.client, ssl, typed protocols, and fixture transports.

hints: For Python Defend the transferred model · Workshop Layered Failure Evidence, name the method, authority, body, byte, time, and credential boundary, Layered Failure Evidence precondition, or invariant that must hold.

At Python Defend the transferred model · Workshop Layered Failure Evidence, trace this concrete input and predict resolution, redirect, status, representation, retry, and cleanup evidence before reviewing it: A workshop civic-data staging importer request P-4 crosses a resolver, TLS connection, gateway, cache, and origin before a changed payload is rejected. Separate every role, representation, state holder, and failure layer before editing code. Competency probe: Classify name resolution, connection, TLS, protocol, status, representation, and business failures without collapsing them. Demonstrate the result through Python 3.14 urllib, http.client, ssl, typed protocols, and fixture transports.

For Python Defend the transferred model · Workshop Layered Failure Evidence, challenge the documented misconception with request, response, fault, policy, and transfer evidence: Every failed operation should be reported as an HTTP status error. This remains false for Python 3.14 urllib and HTTP client layers.
